preamble: Debuccalization of the voiceless alveolar sibilant /s/ to the glottal fricative /h/ is one of the most widespread examples of phonetic weakening in historical linguistics. This process involves the loss of the oral place of articulation (the tongue making contact near the teeth) while preserving the voiceless friction at the glottis. It frequently occurs in syllable-initial or intervocalic positions and often serves as a precursor to total elision.

examples.note: Internal debuccalization in specific morphological contexts (e.g., 'as-tu' vs 'ah-am').
Syllable-final and word-final /s/ debuccalization is a hallmark of southern and coastal Spanish dialects.
PAN *S → PMP *h (then → ∅ in Malay/Indonesian). Cf. Tagalog 'isda' and Malay 'ikan' reflecting different outcomes of *S.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Penny, R. (2002). A History of the Spanish Language.
Sihler, A. L. (1995). New Comparative Grammar of Greek and Latin.
